title: Duodenal ulcer due to external causes
Description: Duodenal ulcer caused by external substances, such as alcohol, radiation, chemical agent and by other external causes.

title: Drug-induced duodenal ulcer
Description: Medications such as NSAIDs can cause duodenal ulcer. Other drugs that may increase the risk of ulceration include potassium chloride, concomitant use of steroids with NSAIDs, bisphosphonates, and mycophenolate mofetil.
Index Terms: Drug-induced duodenal ulcer; Toxic duodenal ulcer; medicament-induced duodenal ulcer; Duodenal ulcer due to NSAID - [non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug]

title: Radiation duodenal ulcer
Index Terms: Radiation duodenal ulcer

title: Chemical duodenal ulcer
Index Terms: Chemical duodenal ulcer

title: Bacterial duodenal ulcer
Description: Duodenal ulcer caused by infection with bacteria. This includes mycobacterium, Treponema pallidum (syphilis bacterium) and other bacterial infections in the duodenum.
Index Terms: Bacterial duodenal ulcer; Duodenal ulcer due to bacterial infections

title: Viral duodenal ulcer
Description: Duodenal ulcer caused by infection with virus. Infectious diseases such as cytomegalovirus and herpes simplex virus are often associated with duodenal ulcer.
Index Terms: Viral duodenal ulcer; Duodenal ulcer due to viral infections

title: Fungal duodenal ulcer
Description: Duodenal ulcer caused by infection with fungus. This includes infection with candida and other fungal infections in the duodenum.
Index Terms: Fungal duodenal ulcer; Duodenal ulcer due to fungal infections

title: Parasitic duodenal ulcer
Index Terms: Parasitic duodenal ulcer; duodenal ulcer due to parasitic infections

title: Nonstructural developmental anomalies of small intestine
Description: Any congenital defect of small intestine that results from interference with the normal growth and differentiation of the fetus. Such defects can arise at any stage of embryonic development, vary greatly in type and severity, and are caused by a wide variety of determining factors, including genetic mutations, chromosomal aberrations, teratogenic agents, and environmental factors. Most developmental defects are apparent at birth, especially any structural malformation, but some becomes evident later.

title: Syndromic diarrhoea
Description: Syndromic diarrhoea (SD), also known as phenotypic diarrhoea (PD) or tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome (THE), is a congenital enteropathy presenting with early-onset of severe diarrhoea requiring parenteral nutrition (PN), associated with facial dysmorphism, woolly and poorly pigmented hair and liver disease, with extensive fibrosis or cirrhosis, in about half of the patients.
Index Terms: Syndromic diarrhoea; Phenotypic diarrhoea; THE - [tricho-hepato-enteric] syndrome

title: Congenital intestinal transport defect
Description: This is a congenital disease of the small intestinal mucosa that presents with intractable diarrhoea and malabsorption of nutrients in young children, due to defect of transporter of nutrients in enterocytes.
Index Terms: Congenital intestinal transport defect; Congenital chloride diarrhoea; Congenital sodium diarrhoea

title: Congenital intestinal motility disorders
Description: This is a congenital disorder of the small intestinal intestine that presents severe impairment or change of bowel movement often associated with malabsorption of nutrients in young children.
Index Terms: Congenital intestinal motility disorders; Chronic idiopathic intestinal pseudo-obstruction; Myopathic intestinal pseudoobstruction; Neuronal intestinal pseudoobstruction; Unclassified intestinal pseudoobstruction; Familial visceral myopathy; Intestinal Congenital motility disorders due to mitochondrial disease; Oculogastrointestinal muscular dystrophy

title: Obstruction of small intestine
Description: Hindrance of the passage of luminal contents in the small intestine. Obstruction of the small intestine can be partial or complete, and caused by intrinsic or extrinsic factors. Simple obstruction is associated with diminished or stopped flow of luminal contents. Strangulating obstruction is associated with impaired blood flow to the small intestine in addition to obstructed flow of luminal contents.
Exclusions: Diverticular disease of small intestine; Crohn disease of small intestine; hernia involving small intestine; ischaemic stricture of small intestine; Paralytic ileus; Intestinal obstruction of newborn

title: Intussusception of small intestine
Description: Intussusception occurs when a segment of bowel invaginates, or telescopes, into adjacent distal bowel, leading to obstruction and possibly ischemic injury.
Index Terms: Intussusception of small intestine; ileocolic intussusception; intussusception NOS

title: Volvulus of small intestine
Description: A volvulus is an abnormal twisting of the intestine around the axis of its own mesentery, resulting in obstruction of the more proximal bowel. Twisting of the mesentery may involve the mesenteric vessels and so make the involved loop particularly susceptible to strangulation and gangrene, with resulting perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis.
Index Terms: Volvulus of small intestine; SBO - [small bowel obstruction] strangulated; small bowel loop; small bowel strangulation

title: Intestinal adhesions or bands of small intestine with obstruction
Description: Small bowel obstruction resulting from intraabdominal adhesion due to laparotomy, trauma, and intraabdominal inflammation such as endometriosis.
Index Terms: Intestinal adhesions or bands of small intestine with obstruction; Postoperative obstruction of the small intestine

title: Obstructive ileus of small intestine due to impaction
Description: Small bowel obstruction may result when a substance such as gallstone or enterolith is too large to traverse the small intestine, especially at the ileocecal valve.

title: Gallstone ileus of small intestine
Description: Small bowel obstruction due to stenosis resulting from impaction of gallstones.
Index Terms: Gallstone ileus of small intestine

title: Enterolith of small intestine
Description: This is a mineral concretion or calculus formed anywhere in the gastrointestinal system, but in this case the small intestine.
Index Terms: Enterolith of small intestine

title: Other acquired anatomical alterations of small intestine
Description: This group incorporates small intestinal disorders principally due to acquired morphological changes of the small intestine, except for obstruction of small intestine (EC).

title: Fistula of small intestine
Description: A small intestinal fistula is defined as an abnormal communication between the small intestine and another epithelialized surface such as the skin or an adjacent loop of bowel.
Index Terms: Fistula of small intestine; Fistula of intestine, site unspecified

title: Pneumatosis intestinalis of small intestine
Description: Pneumatosis intestinalis is a rare condition characterised by multiple, gas-filled cysts, typically within the subserosa and submucosa of the small intestine.
Index Terms: Pneumatosis intestinalis of small intestine

title: Motility disorders of small intestine
Description: Disorders of small intestinal motility due to abnormal contractions, such as weak contractions and disorganised (unsynchronized) contractions. The loss of ability to coordinate motor activity may cause a variety of disorders including small intestinal distention and bacterial overgrowth.

title: Paralytic ileus
Description: A type of ileus, a functional not mechanical obstruction of the small intestines, and a state of pathophysiologic inhibition of motor activity due to non-mechanical causes. The paralysis does not need to be complete, but the intestinal muscles must be so inactive that it leads to a functional blockage of the intestine.
Exclusions: Gallstone ileus of small intestine; Obstructive ileus of small intestine due to impaction
Index Terms: Paralytic ileus; Paralytic ileus of bowel; adynamic ileus; adynamic intestinal obstruction; ileus NOS; paralysis of bowel; paralysis of intestine; paralytic intestinal ileus; Paralytic ileus of small intestine or colon; Paralytic ileus of small and large intestine; Paralytic ileus of small intestine; Paralytic ileus of large intestine; adynamic colon ileus; adynamic colonic ileus; paralysis of colon

title: Noninfectious enteritis or ulcer of small intestine
Description: Noninfectious enteritis and ulcer of small intestine is inflammation or tissue defect in the small intestine of non-infectious origin, usually due to medication including chemotherapy or radiation therapy side effects; or allergic or systemic disorders. Its severity may vary from mild and inconvenient to severe and life-threatening.
Inclusions: Noninfectious small intestinal inflammation, erosion, ulcer or ulcer scar
Exclusions: Crohn disease of small intestine; Functional diarrhoea; Noninfectious neonatal diarrhoea

title: Primary ulcer of small intestine
Description: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine of unknown origin

title: Primary nonspecific ulceration of small intestine
Description: Primary or simple ulcer of the small intestine occurring beyond the duodenum is rare. The lesion includes single ulcers within the jejunum and ileum of unknown etiology. Non-specific ulcer accompanied with Behcet disease is classified elsewhere.
Index Terms: Primary nonspecific ulceration of small intestine; Simple ulcer of small intestine

title: Chronic non-specific multiple ulcers of small intestine
Description: In CNSU patients chronic non-specific multiple ulcers are predominantly found in the ileum, which are circular or irregular in shape. The margins of ulcers are always clear and the intervening mucosa appears normal. CNSU is often characterised by anaemia and hypoalbuminemia due to bleeding and protein loss from multiple ulcers.
Index Terms: Chronic non-specific multiple ulcers of small intestine; CNSU - [Chronic non-specific multiple ulcers] of small intestine

title: Cryptogenic multifocal ulcerous stenosing enteritis
Description: CMUSE is an independent, rare disease characterised by chronic diarrhoea and by non-specific small intestinal ulceration and ulcerative stenosis which responds to corticosteroid therapy.
Index Terms: Cryptogenic multifocal ulcerous stenosing enteritis; CMUSE - [Cryptogenic multifocal ulcerous stenosing enteritis]

title: Drug-induced or toxic enteritis of small intestine
Description: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to medication including mucosal damaged agents such as NSAIDs, aspirin and antibiotics, due to chemotherapy and due to chemical toxic substances.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic enteritis of small intestine; Toxic ileitis; toxic ileal inflammation; toxic necrotic ileitis; Toxic jejunitis

title: Allergic or dietetic enteritis of small intestine
Description: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to allergic disorders including food allergy. This category includes both immediate-type (IgE mediated) and non-IgE-mediated intestinal hypersensitivity, and eosinophilic disorders of small intestine. Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome (EPIES) is also included here.

title: IgE mediated allergic enteritis of small intestine
Description: Immediate type (IgE-mediated) enteric hypersensitivity due to exposure to an allergen in individuals previously sensitized. The symptoms are acute abdominal pain and diarrhoea and can be combined to other symptoms in cases of anaphylaxis.
Index Terms: IgE mediated allergic enteritis of small intestine

title: Eosinophilic enteritis
Description: This refers to a rare and heterogeneous condition of inflammation of small intestine characterised by patchy or diffuse eosinophilic infiltration of the intestinal tissue.
Index Terms: Eosinophilic enteritis; Allergic eosinophilic enteritis

title: Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome
Description: A non-IgE-mediated intestinal hypersensitivity cell-mediated persistent chronic inflammation of the enteric tract which primarily affects children. The most common causal foods are: cow’s milk, soy, rice, oat, meat. In cases of chronic exposure, the most frequent symptoms are emesis, diarrhoea, poor growth and lethargy. In cases of re-exposure after restriction, the patient can present emesis, diarrhoea, hypotension (15%) 2 hours after ingestion.
Index Terms: Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome; FPIES - [Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome]

title: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to other external causes
Description:
Enteritis and ulcer of small intestine induced by external causes, such as foreign body, radiation, trauma, and other external causes. 
Enteritis due to chemical and toxic substances is excluded from here and classified in 'Drug-induced or toxic enteritis of small intestine'.
Exclusions: Drug-induced or toxic enteritis of small intestine

title: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to foreign body
Index Terms: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to foreign body

title: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to radiation
Index Terms: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to radiation

title: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to trauma
Index Terms: Enteritis or ulcer of small intestine due to trauma

title: Coeliac disease
Description: Coeliac disease is a permanent intolerance to gluten proteins, present in wheat, rye, and barley. It is an autoimmune disorder, characterised by a chronic inflammatory state of the small intestinal mucosa and submucosa, which can impair digestion and absorption of nutrients, leading to malnutrition.
Inclusions: Gluten-sensitive enteropathy; Idiopathic steatorrhoea; Nontropical sprue
Index Terms: Coeliac disease; Gee disease; Gee-Herter disease; Gluten-sensitive enteropathy; Herter Gee syndrome; Heubner-Herter disease; Idiopathic steatorrhoea; Nontropical sprue; Thaysen's disease; celiac disease; coeliac rickets; Microscopic enteritis; latent coeliac disease; potential coeliac disease; Non-refractory coeliac sprue; Refractory coeliac sprue; Adult gluten-induced enteropathy; Childhood gluten-induced enteropathy; Adult idiopathic steatorrhoea; Gee-Thaysen disease; Infantile idiopathic steatorrhoea; Childhood coeliac disease; CCD - [childhood coeliac disease]; coeliac infantilism; infant coeliac disease; infantile coeliac disease; infantile non-tropical sprue; non-tropical sprue of childhood; Adult coeliac disease; ACD - [adult coeliac disease]

title: Intestinal malabsorption or protein-losing enteropathy
Description:
Intestinal malabsorption is a diseased condition in which absorption of food nutrients across the intestinal tract is disturbed. Impairment of single or multiple nutrients may lead to malnutrition. 
 Protein-losing enteropathy is a diseased condition in which there is excessive loss of plasma protein into the intestine. More loss of proteins than synthesis may lead to hypoalbuminemia.

title: Intestinal malabsorption
Description: Intestinal malabsorption (syndrome) occurs due to pathological interference with the normal physiological sequence of digestion (intraluminal process), absorption (mucosal process), and transport (post-mucosal events) of nutrients in the small intestine. The concept of intestinal failure is a life-threatening severe type of intestinal malabsorption due to short bowel, structural enterocyte defects, and intestinal dysmotility etc.

title: Bacterial overgrowth syndrome
Description: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth syndrome is a term that describes clinical manifestations that occur when poor movement of intestinal contents allows certain normal intestinal bacteria to grow excessively, causing diarrhoea and poor absorption of nutrients (malabsorption). Various etiological processes can disrupt mechanisms that keep the number of these bacteria low. These include structural abnormalities (congenital or surgical) and disorders that cause decreased gastric acidity, reduced peristaltic activity, and mucosal damage or atrophy.
Index Terms: Bacterial overgrowth syndrome; BOS – [bacterial overgrowth syndrome]; SIBO - [small intestine bacterial overgrowth]; small intestine bacterial overgrowth syndrome; Blind loop syndrome; Contaminated small bowel syndrome; Lane syndrome; Stagnant loop syndrome

title: Tropical sprue
Description: Tropical sprue is a syndrome involving the entire small intestine that causes acute or chronic diarrhoea and malabsorption of nutrients of progressive severity that results in malnutrition and anaemia due to folic acid deficiency. The disorder occurs only among persons (mostly adults) who visit, or are residents of, certain tropical and subtropical areas. Histological changes consist of lengthening of the crypt area and broadening and shortening of the villi with chronic inflammation of small intestine. The cause of tropical sprue is not known, but excess levels of certain types of bacteria in the small intestines have been suggested, and antimicrobial therapy may result in cure of the intestinal abnormalities.
Index Terms: Tropical sprue; TS - [tropical sprue]; Tropical steatorrhoea; psilosis; sprue NOS; tropical diarrhoea; tropical enteropathy; tropical steatorrhea

title: Malabsorption or intolerance of specific nutrients
Description: Food intolerance is a term used for difficulty in digesting a food due to various physiological responses associated with a particular food, or compound found. Food intolerance should not be mistaken for food allergy, which is primarily involving the immune reaction against the food.
Index Terms: Malabsorption or intolerance of specific nutrients; Carbohydrate intolerance other than lactose; Malabsorption due to intolerance to carbohydrate; carbohydrate intolerance; carbohydrate malabsorption; disorder of carbohydrate absorption; disturbance of carbohydrate absorption; Intestinal intolerance to fat; Malabsorption due to intolerance to fat; disorder of fat absorption; disturbance of fat absorption; fat malabsorption; intestinal malabsorption of fat; Intolerance to protein nutrients; Intolerance to dietary proteins; Malabsorption due to intolerance to protein; protein absorption disorder; protein intolerance; protein malabsorption; Milk intolerance; Cow milk intolerance; Intolerance to other substances; Intolerance to food additives; Bile acid malabsorption; Idiopathic bile acid malabsorption; Secondary bile acid malabsorption; Intestinal disease due to vitamin absorption anomaly; Malabsorption due to intolerance, not elsewhere classified; chronic steatorrhoea; exudative enteropathy; food intolerance; Malabsorption due to intolerance to of starch; disorder of starch absorption; disturbance of starch absorption; malabsorption of starch; Cow milk enteropathy

title: Short bowel syndrome
Description: Having less than 200 cm of residual small bowel with or without colon in an adult and for children (< 18 yrs), less than 25% of the normal length of intestine for their respective age.
Index Terms: Short bowel syndrome; SBS - [short bowel syndrome]; Secondary short bowel syndrome; short bowel NOS; short gut syndrome; Short bowel syndrome with colon-in-continuity; SBS - [short bowel syndrome] with colon-in-continuity; Short bowel syndrome with colon in continuity; Short bowel syndrome with no colon-in-continuity; SBS - [short bowel syndrome] with no colon-in-continuity; Short bowel syndrome with no colon in continuity

title: Intestinal failure
Description: The reduction of gut function below the minimum necessary for the absorption of macronutrients and/or water and electrolytes, such that intravenous supplementation is required to maintain health and/or growth.
Index Terms: Intestinal failure

title: Protein-losing enteropathy
Description: Protein-losing enteropathy is a syndrome characterised by the severe loss of serum proteins into the intestine. It is not a single disease, but an atypical manifestation of other diseases, which involves intestinal mucosa as well as intestinal blood or lymphatic vessels.
Index Terms: Protein-losing enteropathy; PLE - [protein-losing enteropathy]; Cardiac postprocedural protein losing enteropathy

title: Certain vascular disorders of small intestine
Description: The whole small intestine receives its blood supply from the superior mesenteric artery and the venous drainage is through the portal system via the superior mesenteric vein. Vascular disorders includes lesions in these vessels and capillary.

title: Angiodysplasia of small intestine
Description: Small dilated submucosal vessels of small intestinal mucosa with perforating vessels going through the muscularis mucosae.
Index Terms: Angiodysplasia of small intestine

title: Arteriovenous malformation of small intestine
Description: Vascular malformations of small intestine that result from a localised maldevelopment of part of the primitive vascular plexus and consist of abnormal arteriovenous communications without intervening capillaries. They vary in size, ranging from massive lesions that are fed by multiple vessels to lesions so small that they are hard to identify at arteriography, surgery, or autopsy, but relatively larger than angiodysplasia.
Index Terms: Arteriovenous malformation of small intestine; Dieulafoy lesion of small intestine

title: Vasculitis of mesenteric arteries
Description: A part of systemic vasculitis involving the gastrointestinal tract which causes mesenteric ischemia. This includes systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa, allergic granulomatous vasculitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome), and thromboangitis obliterans (Buerger’s disease).
Index Terms: Vasculitis of mesenteric arteries

title: Varices of small intestine
Description: The dilation of venous plexus in the small intestine.
Index Terms: Varices of small intestine; Portal hypertensive enteropathy

title: Polyps of small intestine
Description: Polyps of small intestine are benign mushroom-like abnormalities of the small intestine that may have a stalk or be flat with a stalk. (Polyps of small intestine are any mass of tissue that arises from the small intestinal wall and protrudes into the lumen.)
Exclusions: Polyposis syndrome; Adenoma of small intestine; Adenomatous polyp of small intestine

title: Hamartoma of small intestine
Description: Hamartoma of small intestine is a non-neoplastic mass of indigenous tissue in the small intestine.
Index Terms: Hamartoma of small intestine; Solitary juvenile polyp of small intestine; Solitary Peutz-Jegher polyp of small intestine

title: Hyperplastic polyp of small intestine
Description: Hyperplastic/metaplastic polyps of small intestine usually result from the abnormal maturation of the mucosal cells of the small intestines and are usually of small size.
Index Terms: Hyperplastic polyp of small intestine

title: Inflammatory fibroid polyp of small intestine
Description: Inflammatory fibroid polyp (IFP) is a rare tumour and it presents either as a solitary large or sessile lesion that arises in the submucosa. It is characterised by spindle and stellate cells set in an inflammatory, myxoid stroma.
Index Terms: Inflammatory fibroid polyp of small intestine

title: Lymphoid hyperplasia of small intestine
Description: Lymphoid hyperplasia of small intestine is a formation of well differentiated lymphoid tissue due to enhanced cell division in the small intestinal mucosa.
Index Terms: Lymphoid hyperplasia of small intestine

title: Appendicitis
Description: Appendicitis is a condition characterised by inflammation of the vermiform appendix.

title: Acute appendicitis
Description: Acute inflammation and enlargement of the vermiform appendix. It has been recognised as one of the most common causes of severe acute abdominal pain worldwide. Most cases require appropriate medical treatment or removal of the inflamed appendix. If untreated, mortality is high, mainly because of the risk of rupture leading to peritonitis and shock. In this category acute appendicitis only due to common bacterial infection is included, and appendicitis due to specific organisms is ruled out from here, and described elsewhere.
Index Terms: Acute appendicitis

title: Acute appendicitis with generalised peritonitis
Description: This is a condition characterised by acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix that is extending into the free, not contained, inflammation of the peritoneum. There is usually a free perforation and surgical treatment is recommended.
Inclusions: Acute appendicitis with free perforation to the abdominal cavity; acute appendicitis with diffuse peritonitis following rupture or perforation
Index Terms: Acute appendicitis with generalised peritonitis; Acute appendicitis with free perforation to the abdominal cavity; Acute appendicitis with free peritonitis; acute appendicitis with diffuse peritonitis following rupture or perforation; appendicitis with perforation and free pus in the peritoneal cavity; perforated appendix with generalised peritonitis

title: Acute appendicitis with localised peritonitis
Description: This condition is characterised by acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix with peritonitis that is contained into an abscess or phlegmone. There is often an underlying covered perforation of appendix with leakage from the appendix lumen.
Inclusions: Acute appendicitis with contained perforation to a localised abscess
Index Terms: Acute appendicitis with localised peritonitis; Acute appendicitis with contained perforation to a localised abscess; Acute appendicitis with localised peritonitis with rupture or perforation; Acute appendicitis with localised peritonitis without rupture or perforation; Acute appendicitis with perforation and contained peritonitis into an abscess; Appendicitis with peritoneal abscess; Perforated appendix with localised peritonitis; appendicitis with abscess; appendicitis with contained peritonitis; appendicitis with rupture; ruptured appendix NOS; Ileocaecal abscess; Pericaecal abscess; Appendicular abscess; Subcaecal abscess; Postcaecal abscess

title: Acute appendicitis without localised or generalised peritonitis
Description: This condition is characterised by acute inflammation of the veriform appendix in that there is no mention about the extent of the peritonitis. Acute appendicitis without peritonitis is included here. Acute appendicitis with no perforation or abscess, and simply phlegmonous or suppurative appendicitis, these can usually be treated conservatively, are also included here.
Index Terms: Acute appendicitis without localised or generalised peritonitis; acute appendicitis NOS; acute appendicitis with no perforation or abscess and no visible pus; acute appendicitis with no specification if perforated or not; acute appendicitis with phlegmone; acute appendix inflammation NOS

title: Chronic appendicitis
Description: A correctly diagnosed non-acute form of appendicitis. Chronic appendicitis is a disorder caused by inflammation of the appendix over a period of time. While acute appendicitis shows the typical manifestation of an inflamed appendix, chronic appendicitis may cause symptoms related to abdominal discomfort or more generalised symptoms. In this category chronic appendicitis only due to common bacterial infection is included, and appendicitis due to specific organisms is ruled out from here, and described elsewhere.
Index Terms: Chronic appendicitis; Rumbling appendicitis; grumbling appendix; healed appendicitis; interval appendicitis; obstructive appendicitis NOS; recurrent appendicitis

title: Certain specified diseases of appendix
Description: Diseases of appendix other than appendicitis or neoplasm. This includes intussusception, mucocele, hyperplasia, appendicular concretions, diverticulum, fistula and other specified diseases of appendix.
Index Terms: Certain specified diseases of appendix

title: Megaloappendix
Description: The vermiform appendix is an organ that can have variable sizes, locations as well as functional potentials. This refers to the longer and the larger appendix than normal size.
Index Terms: Megaloappendix

title: Hyperplasia of appendix
Description: Hyperplasia of appendix is the rapid growth proliferation of normal lymphoid cells that resemble lymphoid tissue.
Index Terms: Hyperplasia of appendix; lymphoid hyperplasia of appendix

title: Appendicular concretions
Description: A condition of the appendix filled with calcification
Index Terms: Appendicular concretions; Appendicolithiasis; concretion of appendix; Faecalith of appendix; appendicular faecalith; stercolith of appendix; Calculus of appendix

title: Diverticulum of appendix, acquired
Description: A condition of an outpouching of a hollow structure of the appendix.
Index Terms: Diverticulum of appendix, acquired; appendicular diverticulum; diverticulum of appendix; Diverticulum of appendix, acquired without complication; Diverticulum of appendix, acquired, with complication

title: Fistula of appendix
Description: A condition of an abnormal passageway between the appendix and neighbour organs.
Index Terms: Fistula of appendix; appendiceal fistula; appendicular fistula; appendix fistula

title: Intussusception of appendix
Description: A condition in which a part of the appendix has invaginated into another section of the appendix. Complete invagination of the appendix into the caecum may progress to a colo-colic and/or ileo-colic intussusception.
Index Terms: Intussusception of appendix; appendicular intussusception

title: Mucocele of appendix
Description: Mucocele of the appendix is a cystic, dilated appendix filled with mucin. Simple mucocele is not a neoplasm and results from chronic obstruction of the proximal lumen, usually by fibrous tissue.
Index Terms: Mucocele of appendix; appendiceal mucocele; appendicular mucocele

title: Obstruction of large intestine
Description: Hindrance of the passage of luminal contents in the large intestine. Obstruction of the large intestine can be partial or complete, and caused by intrinsic or extrinsic factors. Simple obstruction is associated with diminished or stopped flow of luminal contents. Strangulating obstruction is associated with impaired blood flow to the large intestine in addition to obstructed flow of luminal contents.

title: Intussusception of the large intestine
Description: Intussusception occurs when a segment of bowel invaginatetes, or telescopeds, into adjacent distal bowel, leading to obstruction and possibly ischemic injury. Colonic intussusception is a relatively uncommon condition that is most frequent in the early years of life. There are three main varieties: caecocolic, colocolic and sigmoidrectal.
Index Terms: Intussusception of the large intestine; Caecal intussusception

title: Volvulus of large intestine
Description: A volvulus is an abnormal twisting of the intestine around the axis of its own mesentery, resulting in obstruction of the more proximal bowel. Twisting of the mesentery may involve the mesenteric vessels and so make the involved loop particularly susceptible to strangulation and gangrene, with resulting perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis. The classical sites of large intestinal volvulus are the caecum and the sigmoid colon, although there are reports of volvulus of the transverse colon and the splenic flexure.
Index Terms: Volvulus of large intestine; bowel loop; bowel volvulus; colon loop; colon volvulus; knotting of intestine, bowel, or colon; strangulation of intestine, bowel, or colon; torsion of intestine, bowel, or colon; twist of intestine, bowel, or colon; Caecal volvulus; caecal torsion; Sigmoid volvulus

title: Adhesions of large intestine with obstruction
Description: Large bowel obstruction resulting from intraabdominal adhesion due to laparotomy, trauma, and intraabdominal inflammation such as endometriosis.
Index Terms: Adhesions of large intestine with obstruction; Postoperative obstruction of the large intestine

title: Impaction of large intestine
Description: Large bowel obstruction may result when a substance such as gallstone, foreign body, or enterolith, but not faecal, is too large to traverse the large intestine.
Index Terms: Impaction of large intestine; Impaction of large bowel; impaction intestine NOS; Enterolith of large intestine

title: Stenosis of the rectum
Description: Rectal stenosis is defined as narrowing of the rectum.
Index Terms: Stenosis of the rectum; obstructed rectum; rectal obstruction; rectal stenosis; rectal stricture; stricture of rectum

title: Other acquired anatomical alterations of large intestine
Description: This group incorporates acquired large intestinal disorders principally due to morphological changes of the colon and rectum. Diverticular diseases and obstruction of large intestine are classified in GB a GD, respectively.
Exclusions: Diverticular disease of large intestine; Obstruction of large intestine

title: Fistula of large intestine
Description: Fistula of large intestine is defined as an abnormal communication between the large intestine and another epithelialized surface such as the skin, an adjacent organ or an adjacent loop of bowel. Colovesical fistula is the most common, but colovaginal, colocolic, coloileal, colocutaneous, and coloanal fistulae have been described. Fistulas due to Crohn disease are excluded from here.
Index Terms: Fistula of large intestine; Rectal fistula; Fistula of rectum to skin; perineorectal fistula; perirectal fistula; rectum fistula; Fistula of sigmoid colon; abdominosigmoidal fistula

title: Pneumatosis intestinalis of large intestine
Description: This refers to pneumatosis intestinalis in the large intestine, which is a condition characterised by multiple, gas-filled cysts, typically within the subserosa and submucosa of the intestine.
Index Terms: Pneumatosis intestinalis of large intestine

title: Rectal prolapse
Description: Rectal mucosal prolapse refers to abdominal descent of the rectal mucosa. The best recognised site of the mucosal prolapse is the anterior wall of the rectum.
Index Terms: Rectal prolapse; Prolapse of rectal mucosa; RP - [rectal prolapse]; male proctocele; male rectocele; procidentia of rectum; procidentia rectum; proctoptosis; rectal mucosa prolapse; rectum prolapse; Mucosal prolapse syndrome; MOS - [mucosal prolapse syndrome]

title: Motility disorders of large intestine
Description: Disorders of colonic motility due to abnormal contractions, such as spasms and colonic paralysis. The loss of ability to coordinate motor activity may cause a variety of disorders including colonic distention and severe constipation.

title: Pseudo-obstruction of colon
Description: Colonic pseudo-obstruction is a rare condition with symptoms of decreased ability of the colon to push fluid, food and air through like those caused by a colonic obstruction, or blockage. The clinical and radiological findings are often similar to true obstruction, but no true mechanical blockage is found. The symptoms generally includes dyspepsia, chronic constipation and, in the moments where appear abdominal colic. Ogilvie syndrome: acute pseudoobstruction of the colon in severely ill debilitated patients is included here as acute form.
Index Terms: Pseudo-obstruction of colon; bowel pseudo-obstruction; Acute pseudo-obstruction of colon; Ogilvie syndrome; Chronic pseudo-obstruction of colon

title: Slow transit constipation
Description: Slow transit constipation (STC) typically involves the unusually slow passage of luminal contents through the large intestine. This can lead to chronic problems, such as constipation and uncontrollable soiling and could require colectomy.
Index Terms: Slow transit constipation; Colonic inertia; STC - [slow-transit constipation]

title: Megacolon
Description: Megacolon is a descriptive term indicating an abnormal dilation of large intestine. The dilatation is often accompanied by a paralysis of the peristaltic movements of the bowel.

title: Toxic megacolon
Description: Toxic megacolon is an acute form of colonic distension characterised by a very dilated colon, accompanied by abdominal distension (bloating), and sometimes fever, abdominal pain, or shock. "Toxic" means that this complication occurs with inflammation. Toxic megacolon is a rare, life-threatening widening of the large intestine that occurs within a few days, occurring as a complication of inflammatory bowel disease, such as ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, or colitis of infectious origin.
Index Terms: Toxic megacolon; toxic dilatation of colon; toxic functional megacolon; Toxic megacolon due to Crohn disease; Toxic megacolon due to ulcerative colitis

title: Acquired hypoganglionosis of large intestine
Description: Acquired hypoganglionosis is characterised as a degeneration of ganglion cells and gliosis histologically. The prognosis is usually good following resection of the affected bowel.
Index Terms: Acquired hypoganglionosis of large intestine

title: Certain noninfectious colitis or proctitis
Description: Noninfectious colitis and proctitis is inflammation or tissue defect in the large intestine of non-infectious origin, but not included in inflammatory bowel diseases, including specific type of colitis, colitis due to medication including chemotherapy or radiation therapy side effects; or allergic or systemic disorders.
Exclusions: Inflammatory bowel diseases; Gastroenteritis or colitis of infectious origin

title: Primary ulcer of colon
Description: A condition with single or multiple ulcers (mucosal defect) develops in the rectum or colon. Ulcers of the colon can cause bleeding with straining in people with chronic constipation.
Index Terms: Primary ulcer of colon; Simple ulcer of colon; Rectal ulcer, not elsewhere classified; rectal ulcer NEC; Solitary rectal ulcer syndrome; Ulcer in Behcet disease

title: Microscopic colitis
Description: A condition of inflammation of the colon that is only detectable when the colon's lining is examined under a microscope. The endoscopic appearance of the inner colon lining is normal. There are two types of microscopic colitis: lymphocytic colitis and collagenous colitis.

title: Collagenous colitis
Description: Collagenous colitis is characterised by chronic watery diarrhoea, normal radiological and endoscopic appearance of the colon, and a specific histopathological feature consisting in the presence of a subepithelial collagen band (10 mm or more) adjacent to the basal membrane, together with epithelial lymphocyte infiltration and chronic inflammation of the lamina propria.
Index Terms: Collagenous colitis

title: Lymphocytic colitis
Description: Lymphocytic colitis is an intestinal inflammatory disorder characterised by increased intraepithelial lymphocytes and manifesting as chronic watery diarrhoea, abdominal pain, nausea, incontinence and faecal urgency. Together with collagenous colitis it makes up a group known as microscopic colitis.
Index Terms: Lymphocytic colitis

title: Allergic or dietetic colitis
Description: Colitis and proctitis due to allergic disorders including food allergy. This category includes both immediate-type (IgE mediated) and non-igE-mediated colonic hypersensitivity, and eosinophilic disorders of large intestine. Food protein-induced proctitis/colitis of infants is also included here.

title: Food protein-induced proctitis or colitis of infants
Description: A non-IgE-mediated intestinal hypersensitivity (in the absence of IgE antibodies) induced by food protein that induces inflammatory response mainly to the rectum and distal sigmoid colon. This is a condition in infants that results from an immune response triggered by proteins in the diet mainly milk (through breast-feeding). Patients usually appear healthy and have normal, soft stools that contain flecks or streaks of blood. The diagnosis is usually made based upon the resolution of symptoms upon withdrawal of the presumed food antigen. The usual onset is in the first 2 months of life but it also occurs in infants from 1 day to 6 months of age.
Index Terms: Food protein-induced proctitis or colitis of infants; Food protein–induced allergic proctocolitis

title: Diversion colitis
Description: An inflammatory condition that occurs in segments of the colon and rectum that are diverted from the faecal stream by surgery.
Index Terms: Diversion colitis

title: Colitis or proctitis due to external causes
Description: Colitis and proctitis induced by external causes, such as chemical and toxic substances, foreign body, radiation, trauma, and other external causes.
Exclusions: Gastroenteritis or colitis of infectious origin

title: Chemical colitis or proctitis
Description: This refers to condition of inflammation of large intestine caused by chemical or toxic substances.
Index Terms: Chemical colitis or proctitis; toxic enterocolitis

title: Radiation-induced colitis
Description: This refers to inflammation of the large intestine and colonic ulcer due to radiation.
Index Terms: Radiation-induced colitis; Radiation induced colonic ulcer

title: Radiation proctitis
Description: Radiation proctitis is a rare rectal disease directly induced by pelvic radiotherapy and characterised by rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, tenesmus and sepsis.
Index Terms: Radiation proctitis; radiation-induced proctitis

title: Drug-induced colitis
Description: Colitis or ulcer of large intestine due to medication including mucosal damaged agents such as NSAIDs, antibiotics, chemotherapy drugs, and other medications.
Index Terms: Drug-induced colitis; Drug-induced colonic ulcer; Drug-induced proctitis; medicament-induced colitis; Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug-induced colitis; Other specified drug-induced colitis; Acute haemorrhagic colitis due to drug; Colitis due to antineoplastic drug

title: Certain vascular disorders of large intestine
Description: The whole large intestine receives its blood supply from colonic branches of the superior mesenteric artery and the inferior mesenteric artery, and the venous drainage is through the portal system via the superior and inferior mesenteric vein. Vascular disorders includes lesions in these vessels and capillary.

title: Angiodysplasia of colon
Description: Small dilated submucosal vessels of colonic mucosa with perforating vessels going through the muscularis mucosae.
Index Terms: Angiodysplasia of colon; AV - [angiodysplasia malformation of colon]; angiodysplasia; angiodysplasia of large intestine; colon angiodysplasia; colonic angiodysplasia; telangiectasia of colon; vascular ectasia of colon; vascular ectasia of large intestine; Angiodysplasia of cecum

title: Arteriovenous malformation of large intestine
Description: Vascular malformations of large intestine that result from a localised maldevelopment of part of the primitive vascular plexus and consist of abnormal arteriovenous communications without intervening capillaries. They vary in size, ranging from massive lesions that are fed by multiple vessels to lesions so small that they are hard to identify at arteriography, surgery, or autopsy, but relatively larger than angiodysplasia.
Index Terms: Arteriovenous malformation of large intestine; Dieulafoy vascular malformation of large intestine

title: Vasculitis of large intestine
Index Terms: Vasculitis of large intestine

title: Varices of large intestine
Description: Abnormally dilated veins in the lining of large intestine. Intestinal varices represent varices at an unusual site in patients with cirrhosis and portal hypertension.
Index Terms: Varices of large intestine; Portal hypertensive colonopathy

title: Acute haemorrhagic rectal ulcer
Description: Acute haemorrhagic rectal ulcer is regarded as an acute ischemic mucosal disorder that occurs in elderly patients who are suffering from incipient blood flow reduction due to arteriosclerotic factors. These patients are often bedridden, and this causes a decrease in mucosal blood flow in the lower rectum, which in turn triggers this pathology.
Index Terms: Acute haemorrhagic rectal ulcer; AHRU - [acute haemorrhagic rectal ulcer]

title: Polyp of large intestine
Description: Polyps are abnormal growths rising from the lining of the large intestine that protrude into the intestinal lumen. Polyps can cause bleeding, and over time, can develop into cancers.
Exclusions: Polyposis syndrome; Malignant neoplasms of colon

title: Hyperplastic polyp of large intestine
Description: Hyperplastic polyps are truly benign growths, possessing no potential for progression to colorectal cancer. Hyperplastic polyps pathologically lack dysplasia.
Inclusions: Hyperplastic nodule of large intestine; Serrated polyp of large intestine
Index Terms: Hyperplastic polyp of large intestine; Hyperplastic nodule of large intestine; Serrated polyp of large intestine

title: Inflammatory polyp of large intestine
Description: Inflammatory polyps occur as a result of the chronic inflammation that takes place in the colon and rectum.
Index Terms: Inflammatory polyp of large intestine; colitis polyposa; inflammatory polyps of colon NOS; pseudopolyposis of colon

title: Benign lymphoid polyp of large intestine
Description: Benign lymphoid polyps are associated with hyperplasia (enhanced cell division) of lymphoid tissue in the colonic mucosa. It is seen in parts of the intestine where lymphoid tissues are concentrated like the ileum of the small intestine or rectum.
Index Terms: Benign lymphoid polyp of large intestine; Focal lymphoid hyperplasia

title: Hamartomatous polyp of large intestine
Description: The hamartomatous polyp is a non-neoplastic, benign tumour-like malformation resulting from an abnormal formation of normal tissue. It contains mesenchymal elements of excess vascular and/or fibrous stroma and glandular proliferation with cystic dilatation.
Index Terms: Hamartomatous polyp of large intestine; Solitary juvenile polyp of large intestine; Solitary Peutz-Jegher polyp of large intestine

title: Inflammatory fibroid polyp of large intestine
Description: Inflammatory fibroid polyp is a benign, non-encapsulated submucosal lesion, composed mainly of loose connective tissues, vessels and with an eosinophilic inflammatory component.
Index Terms: Inflammatory fibroid polyp of large intestine

title: Certain infections of the large intestine
Colonic abscess
Description: A condition of the colon, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by focal accumulation of purulent material in colonic tissue. This disease may present with back pain, abdominal pain, fever, rectal bleeding, or diarrhoea. Confirmation is commonly by advanced imaging.
Index Terms: Colonic abscess; paracolic abscess; pericolic abscess

title: Rectal abscess
Description: A condition of the rectum, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material in the rectal area.
Index Terms: Rectal abscess; pararectal abscess; periproctic abscess; rectal boil; rectum abscess; retrorectal abscess

title: Perirectal abscess
Description: A condition of the perirectal region, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by focal accumulation of purulent material within the perirectal region. This condition may present with perirectal pain and swelling, fever, chills, or constipation. Confirmation is commonly by advanced imaging.
Exclusions: Streptococcal cellulitis of skin; Staphylococcal cellulitis of skin
Index Terms: Perirectal abscess; Perirectal cellulitis

title: Ischiorectal abscess
Description: A condition of the rectum, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material in the ischiorectal space. This condition presents with pain in the perianal region, back pain, swelling, or fever. Confirmation is commonly by advanced imaging.
Index Terms: Ischiorectal abscess; Abscess of ischiorectal fossa; Ischiorectal fistula; ischiorectal fossa fistula; Transsphincteric anal fistula

title: Rectal cellulitis
Index Terms: Rectal cellulitis; rectum cellulitis

title: Fissure or fistula of anal regions
Description: Anal fissure and fistula are the common disorders of anal regions. An anal fissure is a superficial linear tear in the anoderm that is distal to the dentate line. An anal fistula is an inflammatory tract between the anal canal and the skin.

title: Anal fissure
Description: An anal fissure is a linear break or tear in the mucosa that lines the anal canal. It may occur when hard or large stools are passed after defecation and typically cause pain and bright red anal bleeding.
Index Terms: Anal fissure; Acute anal fissure; acute post anal fissure; acute post anus fissure; acute postanal fissure; Chronic anal fissure

title: Anal fistula
Description: Anal fistula is an abnormal communication, hollow tract lined with granulation tissue connecting the primary opening inside the anal canal to a secondary opening in the perineal skin. They are usually associated with anorectal abscesses, and they are thought to be a chronic condition after an abscess evacuation.
Index Terms: Anal fistula; Allingham ulcer; fistula in ano; perianal fistula

title: Anorectal fistula
Index Terms: Anorectal fistula; anorectum fistula

title: Stenosis of anal canal
Index Terms: Stenosis of anal canal; anal stricture; anus occlusion; pectenosis; stricture of anal sphincter

title: Ulcer of anus
Description: Ulcer of anus is tissue defect located in the anal regions, extending beyond the submucosa into the muscularis mucosa.
Inclusions: Solitary ulcer of anus; Stercoral ulcer of anus; Ulcer of anus and rectum
Index Terms: Ulcer of anus; Solitary ulcer of anus; Stercoral ulcer of anus; Ulcer of anus and rectum

title: Anal prolapse
Description: This is a condition in which the rectal tissue looses its internal support and protrudes from the anus to the exterior of the body.
Index Terms: Anal prolapse; Prolapse of anal canal; procidentia anus; procidentia of anus; prolapse of anus

title: Haemorrhoids
Description:
A prolapse of vascular cushions resulting in bleeding and painful swelling in the anal canal. Internal haemorrhoids are swollen veins inside the anal canal and one in a vein of the superior rectal plexus, originating above the pectinate line and covered by mucous membrane. 
Internal haemorrhoids are classified according to the degree of prolapse, although this may not always reflect the severity of symptoms.

title: First degree haemorrhoids
Description: (grade I): First-degree haemorrhoids bulge into the anal canal and sometimes bleed, but do not prolapse through the anus.
Inclusions: Haemorrhoids (bleeding) without prolapse outside of anal canal
Index Terms: First degree haemorrhoids; Grade 1 haemorrhoids; Haemorrhoids (bleeding) without prolapse outside of anal canal; stage I haemorrhoids

title: Second degree haemorrhoids
Description: (grade II): Second-degree haemorrhoids prolapse from the anus during bowel movements but then withdraw back up into the anal canal, spontaneously.
Inclusions: Haemorrhoids (bleeding) that prolapse with straining, but retract spontaneously
Index Terms: Second degree haemorrhoids; Grade II haemorrhoids; Haemorrhoids (bleeding) that prolapse with straining, but retract spontaneously; stage II haemorrhoids

title: Third degree haemorrhoids
Description: (grade III): Third-degree haemorrhoids remain prolapsed unless pushed gently back into the anal canal, and they can be reduced manually.
Inclusions: Haemorrhoids (bleeding) that prolapse with straining, and require manual reduction back inside the anal canal
Index Terms: Third degree haemorrhoids; Haemorrhoids (bleeding) that prolapse with straining, and require manual reduction back inside the anal canal; grade III haemorrhoids; stage III haemorrhoids

title: Fourth degree haemorrhoids
Description: (grade IV): Fourth-degree haemorrhoids cannot be pushed back into the anal canal and permanently prolapsed.
Inclusions: Haemorrhoids (bleeding) with prolapsed tissue that cannot be manually reduced
Index Terms: Fourth degree haemorrhoids; Grade IV haemorrhoids; Haemorrhoids (bleeding) with prolapsed tissue that cannot be manually reduced; stage IV haemorrhoids

title: Perianal venous thrombosis
Description: Extremely painful cherry like lesions under the perianal skin containing clotted blood have been attributed to rupture of a blood vessel with haematoma. However, histology confirmed that these lesions are thrombi lying within the thin-walled vessels of the external anal plexus.
Index Terms: Perianal venous thrombosis; Perianal haematoma (nontraumatic); anal thrombosis; perianal thrombosis; thrombosed external haemorrhoid; thrombosed external pile

title: Residual haemorrhoidal skin tags
Description: This refers to residual small benign tumours that form primarily in areas where the skin forms creases, especially in the anal canal which help with stool control.
Index Terms: Residual haemorrhoidal skin tags; hemorrhoidal tag; skin tags of anus; skin tags of rectum

title: Infections of the anal region
Description: Infections of anal canal caused by various microorganisms including bacteria, virus, fungus, parasite and the other specified agents.

title: Abscess of anal region
Description: A condition of the anal and rectal region, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material in the anal or rectal region.

title: Anal abscess
Description: A condition of the anus, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material in the perianal crypts or glands. This condition may present with pain and swelling in the perianal region, or fever. Confirmation is commonly by rectal examination.
Exclusions: Streptococcal cellulitis of skin; Staphylococcal cellulitis of skin; Intrasphincteric abscess
Index Terms: Anal abscess; Perianal abscess; anus abscess; marginal anus abscess; Anal cellulitis

title: Anorectal abscess
Index Terms: Anorectal abscess; anorectum abscess

title: Intrasphincteric abscess
Description: A condition of the intrasphincteric space, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material between the internal and external anal sphincter. Confirmation is commonly by ultrasonography or advanced imaging.
Index Terms: Intrasphincteric abscess; intermuscular anorectal abscess; submucous anorectal abscess

title: Anal polyp
Description: Abnormal mushroom-like growth sticking out from the epithelium rising from the lining of the anus and anal canal.

title: Inflammatory anal polyp
Description: Inflammatory polyp is an abnormal, mushroom-like growth sticking out from the mucous membrane that lines the anus. This mass is a reaction to some type of chronic inflammation in the anus.
Index Terms: Inflammatory anal polyp

title: Lymphoid polyp
Description: Lymphoid polyp is a benign, focal or diffuse small polypoid lesion composed of well-differentiated lymphoid tissue.
Index Terms: Lymphoid polyp

title: Hypertrophied anal papillae
Description: The enlargement of existing anal papillae is a consequence of chronic inflammation and fibrotic proliferation within the anorectal zone, which is known as hypertrophied or fibrous anal polyp.
Index Terms: Hypertrophied anal papillae; fibrous anal polyp

title: Certain specified diseases of anal canal
Index Terms: Certain specified diseases of anal canal

title: Anal spasm
Description: Spasm of the anal sphincter muscle.
Index Terms: Anal spasm; anorectal spasm; proctospasm; rectal spasm

title: Infectious liver disease
Exclusions: Clonorchiasis; Echinococcus infection of liver; Mumps hepatitis

title: Abscess of liver
Description: A condition of the liver, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This condition is characterised by a focal accumulation of purulent material in the liver. This condition may present with fever, abdominal pain, or shock. Confirmation is by advanced imaging or ultrasonography.
Index Terms: Abscess of liver; Hepatic abscess NOS; Bacterial liver abscess; Pyogenic liver abscess; pyogenic hepatic abscess; Cholangitic liver abscess; cholangitic hepatic abscess; Haematogenic liver abscess; haematogenic hepatic abscess; Lymphogenic liver abscess; lymphogenic hepatic abscess; Capillariasis due to Capillaria hepatica; hepatic capillariasis; Periportal abscess; Pylephlebitic hepatic abscess; pylephlebitis hepatic abscess

title: Acute or subacute hepatic failure
Description: Acute and subacute liver failure is characterised by onset of coagulopathy and/or hepatic encephalopathy within 8 weeks of onset of symptoms.
Exclusions: Liver disorders in pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium; Liver transplant failure; Hepatic encephalopathy; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease; Chronic viral hepatitis; Alcoholic hepatitis; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver with hepatic encephalopathy; Hepatic failure complicating abortion, ectopic or molar pregnancy; Icterus of fetus and newborn; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease with hepatic encephalopathy; Chronic hepatic failure due to portosystemic shunt

title: Acute or subacute hepatic failure due to hepatitis virus
Description: Acute infection of hepatitis A, B, C, D and E viruses can cause acute and subacute hepatic failure. The prognosis varies depending on the virus.
Index Terms: Acute or subacute hepatic failure due to hepatitis virus; Acute and subacute fulminant hepatitis due to other hepatitis virus; Acute hepatitis with hepatic failure; Acute or subacute fulminant hepatitis due to hepatitis A virus; Acute or subacute fulminant hepatitis due to hepatitis B virus; Acute or subacute fulminant hepatitis due to Hepatitis B virus with delta agent; Acute or subacute fulminant hepatitis due to hepatitis E virus

title: Other acute or subacute hepatic failure
Description: Other causes of acute and subacute hepatic failure are drugs, toxic agents, metabolic diseases (particularly Wilson’s disease), ischemic diseases, autoimmune hepatitis, and unknown diseases.
Index Terms: Other acute or subacute hepatic failure; Acute hypoxic liver failure; Acute ischaemic liver failure; Acute metastatic liver failure; Acute and subacute hepatic failure due to autoimmune hepatitis; Acute and subacute hepatic failure due to drug or toxic agent

title: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
Description: NAFLD is characterised by fatty liver related to insulin resistance in the absence of significant alcohol consumption. It embraces a pathological spectrum from simple steatosis to steatohepatitis. 10-20% of cases have steatohepatitis (non-alcoholic steatohepatitis: NASH), which can progress to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
Exclusions: inherited defects in mitochondrial metabolism; Reye syndrome; Acute fatty liver of pregnancy; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease; Chronic hepatitis C; Alcoholic liver disease

title: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis
Index Terms: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis; NAFLD without NASH; NAFLD without mention of NASH; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without mention of non-alcoholic steatohepatitis; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, with cirrhosis; NAFLD without NASH, with cirrhosis; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with fibrosis; hepatic fibrosis due to non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, with cirrhosis and fibrosis; NAFLD without NASH, without mention of cirrhosis; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease without non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, without cirrhosis or fibrosis; NAFLD without NASH, without cirrhosis

title: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis
Description: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is a histological form of Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) in which the key features are histological evidence of hepatocyte injury (such as ballooning or Mallory hyaline) and substantial lobular inflammation. NASH is often associated with fibrosis in pericentral and perisinusoidal distribution (a portal fibrosis pattern also exists, particularly in children). NASH is the clinically progressive form of NAFLD with clinical outcomes including cardiovascular events, and cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma. While novel biomarkers for NASH have been reported, histology remains the gold standard for diagnosis.
Index Terms: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis; NASH - [non-alcoholic steatohepatitis]; non-alcoholic steatohepatosis; Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with cirrhosis; NASH with cirrhosis; Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with fibrosis; NASH without cirrhosis; Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis without cirrhosis or fibrosis; NASH without mention of cirrhosis

title: Hepatic fibrosis or cirrhosis
Exclusions: Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis; Cardiac fibrosis and cirrhosis of liver; Alcoholic liver fibrosis; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without hepatitis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver; Fibropolycystic liver disease; Congenital hepatic fibrosis; Chronic viral hepatitis with cirrhosis; Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis; Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease; Cardiac cirrhosis
Index Terms: Hepatic fibrosis or cirrhosis; fibrosis and cirrhosis of liver

title: Hepatic fibrosis
Description: Hepatic Fibrosis is defined as an excess deposition of the components of extracellular matrix (i.e. collagens, glycoproteins, proteoglycans) within the liver. This response to liver injury potentially is reversible. In contrast, in most patients, cirrhosis is not a reversible process.
Inclusions: hepatic fibrosis with hepatic sclerosis; hepatic sclerosis
Exclusions: Alcoholic liver fibrosis; Congenital hepatic fibrosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver
Index Terms: Hepatic fibrosis; fibrosis of liver; fibrotic liver; hepatic fibrosis NOS; hepatic fibrosis with hepatic sclerosis; hepatic sclerosis; liver fibrosis; liver fibrosis NOS

title: Hepatic cirrhosis
Description: Hepatic (liver) cirrhosis is the end stage of fibrosis of the liver caused by many kinds of liver diseases and conditions. Diffuse nodulation of liver due to fibrous bands subdividing liver into regenerative nodules. Blood vessels reach outflow through resistant collagen which contributes to the portal hypertension. Liver cirrhosis is usually irreversible. Some patients with cirrhosis in the early stage are asymptomatic, and other patients in the advanced stage showed signs and symptoms caused by decreased hepatic synthetic function, portal hypertension or decreased detoxification function.
Exclusions: Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis; Congenital cirrhosis of liver; Secondary biliary cirrhosis; Cardiac fibrosis and cirrhosis of liver; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without hepatitis
Index Terms: Hepatic cirrhosis; cirrhosis NOS; hepatic cirrhosis NOS; liver cirrhosis; liver cirrhosis NOS; Decompensated liver cirrhosis

title: Certain specified fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver
Description: This is other formation of excess fibrous connective tissue in an organ or tissue in a reparative or reactive process and chronic liver disease characterised by replacement of liver tissue by fibrosis, scar tissue and regenerative nodules.
Index Terms: Certain specified fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver; Biliary cirrhosis, unspecified

title: Hereditary North American Indian childhood cirrhosis
Description: Hereditary North American Indian childhood cirrhosis is a severe autosomal recessive intrahepatic cholestasis that has only been described in aboriginal children from northwestern Quebec. Manifesting first as transient neonatal jaundice, the disease evolves into periportal fibrosis and cirrhosis during a period ranging from childhood to adolescence.
Index Terms: Hereditary North American Indian childhood cirrhosis

title: Idiopathic copper-associated cirrhosis
Description: Idiopathic copper-associated cirrhosis is a rare copper-overload liver disease characterised by a rapidly progressive liver cirrhosis from the first few years of life leading to hepatic insufficiency and harbouring a specific pathological aspect: pericellular fibrosis, inflammatory infiltration, hepatocyte necrosis, absence of steatosis, poor regeneration and histochemical copper staining.
Index Terms: Idiopathic copper-associated cirrhosis; Non-Wilsonian hepatic copper toxicosis of infancy and childhood; Tyrolean infantile cirrhosis; Idiopathic copper toxicosis; Indian childhood cirrhosis

title: Alcoholic liver disease
Description: Alcoholic liver disease is damage to the liver and its function due to excessive intake of alcohol over a prolonged period of time. The diagnosis is made by a history of excessive intake of alcohol and exclusion of other causes of liver disease. However, it is important to note that excessive alcohol intake interacts with other causes of chronic liver disease to worsen the pathological severity and clinical outcome; important (relatively common) examples are with chronic hepatitis C, obesity and diabetes-related fatty liver, and haemochromatosis.

title: Alcoholic fatty liver
Description: Alcoholic fatty liver is abnormal retention of lipids in liver cells evident as stainable fat (steatosis) due to excessive intake of alcohol. It is the earliest stage of alcoholic liver disease. It is difficult to histologically distinguish alcoholic fatty liver from non-alcoholic fatty liver. Diagnosis is dependent on history of level of alcohol intake (see definition of NAFLD (Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease)).
Index Terms: Alcoholic fatty liver; ETOH fatty liver; ETOH fatty liver metamorphosis; alcoholic fatty liver disease; fatty ETOH liver necrosis; Zieve syndrome

title: Alcoholic hepatitis
Description: Alcoholic hepatitis is injury and inflammation of the liver caused by excessive intake of alcohol. It is characterised by infiltration by neutrophils, ballooning degeneration of hepatocytes and deposit of Mallory hyaline bodies. Alcoholic hepatitis often occurs concomitantly in patients with other forms of alcoholic liver disease such as fatty liver (alcoholic steatohepatitis), liver fibrosis and cirrhosis.

title: Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis
Description: This is an inflammation of the liver due to excessive intake of alcohol with cirrhosis, a liver disease characterised by replacement of liver tissue by fibrosis, scar tissue and regenerative nodules.
Index Terms: Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver with hepatitis; Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis, with complications of cirrhosis; Alcoholic hepatitis with cirrhosis, without complications of cirrhosis

title: Alcoholic liver fibrosis
Description: Alcoholic fibrosis of liver is defined as an excess deposition of the collagens and extracellular matrix within the liver as evident histologically, caused by excessive intake of alcohol.
Index Terms: Alcoholic liver fibrosis; Alcoholic sclerosis of liver; alcohol liver fibrosis; alcoholic fibrosis and sclerosis of liver

title: Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without hepatitis
Description: Alcoholic cirrhosis is an advanced pathological stage of alcoholic liver disease characterised by diffuse fibrosis that links portal tracts and central veins, distortion of the hepatic architecture and the formation of regenerative nodules.
Index Terms: Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without hepatitis; Alcoholic cirrhosis NOS; ETOH cirrhosis; Laennec cirrhosis; alcohol cirrhosis; alcohol hepatic cirrhosis; alcohol liver cirrhosis; alcoholic Laennec cirrhosis; alcoholic cirrhosis; alcoholic liver cirrhosis; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver with complications; Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without complications; Alcohol macronodular cirrhosis; alcoholic macronodular cirrhosis; Alcohol micronodular cirrhosis; alcoholic micronodular cirrhosis; Alcohol portal cirrhosis; alcoholic portal cirrhosis; Alcohol postnecrotic cirrhosis; alcoholic postnecrotic cirrhosis; Alcoholic nodular cirrhosis; ETOH nodular cirrhosis; Nutritional alcoholic cirrhosis

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease
Description: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease is hepatotoxicity as injury to the liver that is associated with impaired liver function caused by exposure to a drug or another noninfectious agent.

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with acute hepatic necrosis or acute hepatitis
Description: This is an acute hepatocellular injury that develops within 1 to 20 weeks after starting treatment. The histological lesions consist mainly of focal, zonal or bridging necrosis, but lobular and portal tract inflammation evident with drug hepatitis. Extrahepatic features of drug hypersensitivity, including rash, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia or other systemic features, are observed in some cases.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with acute hepatic necrosis or acute hepatitis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with acute hepatic necrosis or acute hepatitis, with complications; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with acute hepatic necrosis or acute hepatitis, without complications

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis
Description: This is the chronic form of drug-induced liver injury that almost always depends on continued exposure to the agent, and is characterised by interface hepatitis, bridging necrosis, and fibrosis.

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis with cirrhosis
Description: This is the chronic form of drug-induced liver injury characterised by interface hepatitis, bridging necrosis and fibrosis, with the development of cirrhosis.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis with cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis, with cirrhosis, with complications of cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis, with cirrhosis, without complications of cirrhosis

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis without cirrhosis
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic hepatitis without cirrhosis

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis
Description: This is a drug-induced cholestatic liver injury where the symptoms of pruritus and jaundice are often prominent, and elevated serum alkaline phosphatase is the dominant biochemical finding.

title: Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis
Description: This is a chronic cholestasis defined by persistence of drug-induced cholestatic liver injury for more than 3 months.
Index Terms: Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis; Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis, with cirrhosis; Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis, with cirrhosis, with complications of cirrhosis; Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis, with cirrhosis, without complications of cirrhosis; Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis, without cirrhosis; Chronic drug-induced or toxic liver disease with cholestasis, without mention of cirrhosis

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fatty liver
Description: This is a drug-induced fatty liver that consists of three types of steatosis, including microvesicular steatosis, macrovesicular steatosis, and phospholipidosis.

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease
Description: This is a chronic drug-induced steatosis characterised by relatively large triglyceride globules, which effectively fill the hepatocyte, displace the nucleus and other intracellular constituents to the periphery. Some patients show steatohepatitis, steatosis with focal necrosis and inflammatory cell infiltrate, leading to the development of cirrhosis.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease, with cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease, with cirrhosis, with complications of cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease, with cirrhosis, without complications of cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease, without cirrhosis; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with chronic fatty liver disease, without mention of cirrhosis

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with granulomatous hepatitis
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with granulomatous hepatitis; toxic liver disease with hepatic granuloma

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver
Description: This is a drug-induced hepatic fibrosis as the end result of chronic hepatitis, chronic hepatotoxicity, steatohepatitis, or chronic cholestasis with bile duct injury.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver, with complications; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with fibrosis or cirrhosis of liver, without complications

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with vascular disorders of the liver
Description: Drug-associated disorders, leading to portal hypertension independent of primary liver disease, include hepatic vein thrombosis, hepatic veno-occlusive disease, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and nodular regenerative hyperplasia.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with vascular disorders of the liver; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with vascular disorders of the liver, with complications; Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with vascular disorders of the liver, without complications

title: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with liver tumours
Description: Medication and chemical exposure have been associated with many forms of hepatic neoplasms, including focal nodular hyperplasia, hepatic adenoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma and angiosarcoma.
Index Terms: Drug-induced or toxic liver disease with liver tumours

title: Autoimmune liver disease
Description: Autoimmune liver diseases are generally forms of chronic liver disease in which the etiology is unclear but autoimmune mechanisms are evident or postulated for the development of the disease. The primary target organ is the liver and/or biliary system. It can progress to liver cirrhosis.

title: Autoimmune hepatitis
Description: Autoimmune hepatitis is a chronic hepatitis, which can progress to liver cirrhosis, generally featured by the presence of circulating autoantibodies and hyperglobulinemia. It has been subdivided into three main categories according to the autoantibodies detected.

title: Budd-Chiari syndrome
Description:
Budd-Chiari syndrome (BCS) is caused by obstruction of hepatic venous outflow involving either the hepatic veins or the terminal segment of the inferior vena cava and leading to hepatic congestion and ischemic necrosis. Severity depends on the speed of onset and extent of the obstruction. 
Obstructions are generally caused by thrombosis in primary BCS, while secondary BCS results from tumour invasion into the lumen or compression of the vein by an expansive lesion. The principle manifestations of BCS are ascites leading to undernutrition and renal insufficiency, gastrointestinal haemorrhage due to portal hypertension, and hepatic insufficiency resulting in encephalopathy and severe infections. Asymptomatic forms have also been reported.

title: Hepatic encephalopathy
Description: Hepatic encephalopathy is a complication of liver cirrhosis and a hallmark of acute liver failure, and is also observed in patients with portosystemic shunts without cirrhosis. Hepatic encephalopathy is characterised by personality changes, intellectual impairment, flapping tremor and a decreased consciousness level. In the advanced stages it is called hepatic coma, which may lead to death. The diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy is made primarily by recognition of neuropsychiatric changes occurring in a patient with liver disease, after exclusion of brain diseases.

Small intestinal diverticula are far less common than colonic diverticula. Most people with diverticulosis do not have any discomfort or symptoms. However, some people may experience pain or discomfort in the abdomen, bloating, and bleeding.

title: Non-occlusive mesenteric ischaemia
Description: Non-occlusive mesenteric ischaemia causes 20% to 30% of acute mesenteric ischaemia episodes. Mesenteric ischaemia without anatomic arterial or venous obstruction is due to mesenteric vasospasm, which can occur during periods of relatively low mesenteric flow, especially if there is underlying arterial atherosclerotic disease. Such low-flow state can result from heart failure, hypotension, or hypovolemia.

title: Ischaemic colitis
Description: Ischemic colitis is the most common form of ischemic injury to the gut and occurs more frequently in elderly people. The disease can result from either occlusive or nonocclusive events, mainly in the territory of the inferior mesenteric artery, in colonic branches of the superior mesenteric artery, and in the superior and inferior mesenteric veins. The splenic flexure and rectosigmoid junction, where low perfusion exists, are commonly affected.
